ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a water chestnut food product class; the sweetened beverage form is not separately enumerated. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers water chestnut as a commodity; no dedicated sweetened beverage sub-code exists. Closest parent match used at commodity level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for water chestnut beverage. Null retained to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one cup (approximately 250 ml / 250 g) of water chestnut drink, as commonly sold at hawker centres and drink stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Water chestnut drink with sugar — HPB Singapore Foodo Database (D05010038)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record D05010038 for water chestnut drink with sugar. All core and secondary macros are exact matches from this source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for sweetened water chestnut drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on: (1) water chestnut (raw) has a moderate GI of approximately 60 per analogous starchy aquatic vegetable data; (2) the beverage form with added sucrose (9.4 g/100 g) would maintain a medium GI range. Sucrose GI is approximately 65 (University of Sydney). The blended estimate of 60 is conservative and defensible. GL per 250 g serving = (60 × 9.4 × 2.5) / 100 = 14.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.4 g/100 g) as a sugar-sweetened beverage; sodium is low at 9 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is low (9 mg/100 g), the 9.4 g/100 g sugar content classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this drink and request an unsweetened (wu tang) version.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: All 9.4 g/100 g carbohydrate is sugar with zero dietary fibre; sugar-sweetened beverage causing rapid glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: This sugar-sweetened beverage delivers 9.4 g of free sugar per 100 ml with no fibre to blunt absorption, producing a rapid glycaemic spike; individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this drink or substitute with an unsweetened water chestnut preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar (9.4 g/100 g) in a liquid form; no saturated fat or cholesterol concern but SSB classification applies
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation and endothelial dysfunction; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this drink and prefer the unsweetened version.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.4 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The 9.4 g/100 g added sugar content promotes inflammatory cytokine production with no offsetting omega-3, polyphenols, or fibre; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sweetened version and opt for unsweetened water chestnut drink.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Water chestnut drink with sugar (马蹄水, Ma Ti Shui) is a traditional Singaporean and Southern Chinese sweetened beverage made by boiling fresh or dried water chestnuts (Eleocharis dulcis) with sugar and water, then straining the liquid. The drink has a light, mildly sweet, and subtly earthy flavour with a pale golden colour. It is commonly sold at hawker centres, drink stalls, and traditional Chinese herbal drink shops across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, per 100 ml it provides 38 kcal, 9.4 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), 0.1 g protein, 0 g fat, and 0 g dietary fibre, with low sodium (9 mg) and small amounts of calcium (3 mg) and iron (0.3 mg). As a sugar-sweetened beverage with all carbohydrate as free sugar and zero fibre, it is classified as high concern for type 2 diabetes and moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The unsweetened version (wu tang / 无糖) is a healthier alternative that retains the mild flavour of water chestnut without the glycaemic burden. Water chestnuts themselves contain antioxidants and have traditionally been used in Chinese medicine as a cooling food, but the added sugar in this commercial preparation negates those benefits.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink water chestnut drink?
How many calories in water chestnut drink?
Is water chestnut drink high in sugar?
Is water chestnut drink good for high blood pressure?
What is the GI of water chestnut drink?
Can I drink water chestnut drink if I have hypertension?
What is the unsweetened version of water chestnut drink called?

water chestnut: water caltrop (distinct species but sometimes confused)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record D05010038, which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. The match is exact. GI is estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database data for water chestnut and sucrose, which is the approved GI source.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo D05010038 — this is a water-based sweetened beverage with no fat-containing ingredients; confirmed zero is appropriate. saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero by the same rationale. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — the water chestnut solids are strained out in the beverage preparation, leaving no measurable fibre in the liquid. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this beverage is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database record D05010038 as of 2026. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for water chestnut and sucrose; no direct GI measurement for sweetened water chestnut drink was found in the approved GI database. GL per 250 g serving calculated as (60 × 9.4 × 2.5) / 100 = 14.1.
